title: Pattern Recognition
narrativeGoal: Sable's private analysis of the signal deepens. She builds a spectrogram, discovers the signal has internal structure — not random, not mechanical. Her diagnostic tools keep returning UNCLASSIFIED. She begins talking to Drone Six while she works, a habit she's stopped noticing.
readerEffect: Feel Sable's expertise meeting its limit — she's very good at this, and it's not enough. The signal should be explainable. It isn't.

knows: Sable is alone on Meridian, day 337+
An anomalous signal appeared during routine maintenance
She didn't report it — saved it as personal data
suspects: Sable's loneliness is affecting her judgment
wrongAbout: That Sable will eventually file the report

knows: The signal has internal mathematical structure
It doesn't match any catalogued phenomenon
Sable talks to Drone Six as a companion
suspects: The signal might be a message
Sable is lonely enough to want it to be a message
wrongAbout: That this is just professional curiosity
activeTensions: Sable's isolation is shaping her analysis

characterKnowledgeChanges.sable: Discovers the signal has non-random internal structure — a periodicity that doesn't match any known natural source. Her analysis tools categorize it as noise, but the spectrogram tells a different story. She begins building custom classification criteria.

subtext.surfaceConversation: An engineer running increasingly sophisticated diagnostics on an anomalous reading
subtext.actualConversation: A lonely person looking for evidence that something out there is paying attention
subtext.enforcementRule: Sable must never acknowledge she wants the signal to be intentional. All motivation must appear technical. The loneliness enters only through behavior — talking to the drone, the hours she's keeping, what she's not eating.

sable: Addresses Drone Six conversationally without noticing she's doing it
Uses station log entries as emotional containment

pacing: Methodical and tightening. Analysis scenes feel like narrowing focus — each test eliminating possibilities until what's left is impossible. Short bursts of action alternating with stillness.
sensoryNotes: The hum of the spectrogram display. Cold coffee. The click of Drone Six's maintenance joints. Blue-shifted light from analysis screens washing out the station's amber.
sceneSpecificProhibitions: No eureka moment — the realization should creep, not arrive
Sable must not use the word 'signal' as if it's already significant — it's still 'the reading' or 'the return' to her
Do not describe Sable's face or expressions — intimate POV, she can't see herself
No internal monologue about loneliness — show it through what she does

anchorLines.text: Noise has no opinions. This had opinions.
anchorLines.placement: Final third, after the spectrogram reveals structure

chunkDescriptions: Station log, day 338. Sable returns to the data. She's set up a private analysis workspace — off the main logs. Drone Six hovers nearby as she works. Coffee she forgot to drink.

The spectrogram. The signal's internal structure becomes visible. It's not random. She runs every classification algorithm she has. Nothing matches. She starts ruling things out — pulsar timing, gravitational lensing, instrument echo. The list of what it isn't grows longer than the list of what it could be.

The quiet after. Sable sits with what she's found. She writes a station log entry that says nothing about it. She tells Drone Six to maintain position on sector eight. She opens a new file, labels it personal, and begins writing her own classification criteria.
failureModeToAvoid: Making Sable seem irrational or reckless. Her protocol violation should feel like the logical choice of someone whose expertise is telling her something her systems can't confirm.
